Jewish Tradition

Maimonides

1138 – 1204

Reason, prophecy, and the perfection of the intellect in the Guide of the Perplexed.

The best reconstruction, in my judgment, is that Maimonides offers a hierarchical, hylomorphic, and strongly intellectualist philosophy of mind. Human mentality is a structured unity of faculties within an embodied living being; its perfection consists in the actualization of intellect, the right governance of appetite, the disciplining of imagination, and the knowledge of God. But this psychology is inseparable from a wider noetic cosmos containing celestial souls, separate intellects or angels, and God as pure intellect. If one removes God and the angels from the picture as “merely theological,” one does not simplify Maimonides; one breaks the architecture of the theory.

A first caution is textual. Maimonides does not give us one tidy treatise called “philosophy of mind.” The relevant doctrine is distributed across the Eight Chapters, the Guide of the Perplexed, the Mishneh Torah—especially Yesodei ha-Torah and Teshuvah—and the Treatise on Resurrection. When these are read together, they yield a coherent but tension-filled account of soul, intellect, imagination, prophecy, providence, and postmortem existence.

A second caution is lexical. Maimonides uses a layered vocabulary: nefesh, ruaḥ, form, knowledge, intellect, and even “angel” do not function as flat synonyms. In Yesodei ha-Torah 4 he explicitly warns that the relevant form is sometimes called nefesh and sometimes ruaḥ, and that one must be careful with the names lest one be misled. That warning matters because many interpretive mistakes begin by assuming that “soul” always means the same thing in every passage.

At the human level, Maimonides begins with a strict unity thesis. In the Eight Chapters he insists that the human soul is one, not many, and that the “parts” of the soul are not bodily pieces but diverse activities of a single whole. He then enumerates five faculties: nutritive, sensitive, imaginative, appetitive, and rational. His medical analogy is deliberate: just as bodily medicine requires a knowledge of the organism and its parts, moral and spiritual healing requires a science of the soul and its faculties.

This already tells us something important about his philosophy of mind. Maimonides is not starting from an inner theater of “mental states” in a modern sense. He is starting from powers or capacities of a living, embodied subject. Mind is functional, teleological, and normatively ordered from the beginning. To ask what a mind is, for him, is immediately to ask what its powers are for and how they can be healthy or diseased.

The most important lower-to-middle faculty is imagination. In the Eight Chapters, imagination retains sensory impressions after the object has disappeared, combines and separates them, and can produce fictions that have no corresponding reality. In the Guide Maimonides sharpens the contrast: intellect analyzes, abstracts, and grasps causal and essential relations, whereas imagination handles individuals and composites, cannot produce a purely immaterial image, and gives no criterion of reality. That is why things may be unimaginable and yet demonstrably true, while other things are imaginable and yet false.

That distinction is not merely epistemic. It structures error. For Maimonides, many philosophical and theological confusions arise because people let imagination rule where intellect should judge. His explicit example is instructive: imagination can represent God corporeally, but that tells us nothing about reality. So imagination is powerful, necessary, and often fertile, yet by itself it is epistemically unreliable.

His moral psychology is equally precise. In the Eight Chapters he says observance and transgression arise chiefly from the sensitive and appetitive faculties, because those are the faculties involved in voluntary action. Nutrition and imagination, by contrast, do not directly ground praise and blame, since their operations continue even in sleep and are not suspended by choice. Yet the rational faculty is not morally neutral either: true and false beliefs also belong to the domain of moral significance, and the intellectual virtues—wisdom, reason, and acquired intellect—belong specifically to the rational faculty.

So Maimonides’ human mind is neither just a reasoning machine nor just a battleground of passions. It is a stratified unity in which appetite, sensation, imagination, and intellect must be ordered. The normative principle of that order is clear in Chapter V of the Eight Chapters: all the powers of the soul should be subordinated to reason and directed toward one goal, namely knowledge of God as far as mortal beings can attain it. Even eating, sleeping, sex, movement, and rest are to be regulated so that the soul and its agencies remain fit for that highest end.

This emphasis on bodily regulation shows why it is too crude to call Maimonides a simple dualist. He repeatedly says that bodily condition affects psychic condition. In the Guide he remarks that the sufferings of the soul follow bodily changes because the soul is a force residing in the body, and in the prophecy chapters he stresses that the imaginative faculty depends on the temperament and constitution of its bodily organ. Even the highest mental states have physiological preconditions.

At the same time, he is not a neutral embodiment theorist. Matter and body are, for him, the usual sources of defect, vice, and cognitive obstruction. In the Guide he says that man’s shortcomings and sins come from bodily substance, while knowledge of God, formation of ideas, and mastery of desire belong to his form. He also says that the corporeal element acts like a partition that blocks the apprehension of immaterial truths. The body is therefore both necessary instrument and principal obstacle.

That two-sided view explains his politics and legal theory. The Law has, he says, a double aim: the well-being of the soul through correct opinions, and the well-being of the body through just social order. The first is higher in rank, but the second is prior in nature and time, because one cannot perfect the soul without first securing bodily and civic conditions. This is a major philosophical point: for Maimonides, mind is perfected neither in isolation nor in raw inwardness, but through an ordered ethical and political world.

One of the most revealing features of his anthropology is what might reasonably be called a recursive or nested hylomorphism. In the Eight Chapters, after describing the soul’s five faculties, Maimonides says that this one soul may itself be compared to matter, and that its form is reason. In Yesodei ha-Torah 4, he says the soul of all flesh is the form given to it by God, but that the “extra” dimension in man is the form of the human being perfected in knowledge. That higher form knows immaterial realities like the angels do.

This is, I think, the key to extracting a comprehensive philosophy of mind from him. The human being is a hylomorphic composite, but within that composite the lower life-soul is not the last word. The human soul is oriented toward an intellective perfection that stands to it somewhat as form stands to matter. Maimonides even says in Yesodei ha-Torah that “knowledge is the form of this dimension of soul.” That is a remarkably strong claim: mind, in the highest human sense, is not merely the possession of faculties but their intellectual actualization.

That point becomes clearer when Maimonides discusses intellect as such. In Guide I.68, he argues that when intellect is actualized, intellect, intelligent, and intelligible are one. In ordinary human knowing this occurs intermittently, when a form is truly understood; in God it occurs always, since God is never potential but always actual. The human intellect therefore participates, in limited and episodic form, in a structure that is fully and eternally realized only in God.

Here the Active Intellect becomes crucial. Maimonides’ language of “overflow” or emanation belongs to a broader Arabic philosophical context in which the Active Intellect is the source of human knowledge and prophecy. In the Guide this influence “enables us to think” and differentiates human intellectual levels; modern scholarship on Maimonides’ Islamic background confirms that the Active Intellect in this tradition is the lowest separate intellect and the source of sublunar intellection and prophecy.

Prophecy is where Maimonides’ philosophy of mind is most fully on display. He defines prophecy as an overflow sent by God through the Active Intellect first to the rational faculty and then to the imaginative faculty. That single sentence gives his entire causal model of higher cognition: divine source, intermediary intellect, rational reception, then imaginative representation. Prophecy is thus neither irrational ecstasy nor arbitrary inspiration. It is an ordered noetic process.

He is also explicit about types. When the influence reaches only the logical faculty, the result is the philosopher or wise person. When it reaches both logical and imaginative faculties, the result is the prophet. When imagination receives influence without sufficient rational development, the result is not genuine prophecy but the lower class of diviners, statesmen, charmers, and people with vivid true dreams mingled with illusion. Here Maimonides is constructing a taxonomy of minds by the relative perfection and coordination of intellect and imagination.

His account is not a purely naturalistic one, but it is close. In Guide II.32 he presents the “philosophers’” view that prophecy is a natural human perfection requiring intellectual, moral, and imaginative excellence, then says that Scripture agrees except for one addition: even a perfectly prepared person may fail to prophesy if God withholds the faculty’s exercise. Prophecy is therefore natural in structure but not mechanically guaranteed. It lies at the junction of perfected nature and divine permission.

The bodily dimension remains present even here. Maimonides insists that prophecy depends not only on moral and rational cultivation but on the excellent natural constitution of the imaginative organ. That detail matters philosophically: higher cognition is not indifferent to embodiment. Yet the very same discussion says that dreams and prophecy differ only by degree, not by kind. Imagination, then, is the ordinary bridge between sense and the supersensible—dangerous if left alone, indispensable when disciplined and elevated.

Moses is the decisive exception and therefore the limit case. Maimonides repeatedly says that all prophets except Moses receive prophecy through an angel and through the imaginative faculty, whereas Moses received it without that medium. In the Guide Moses’ prophecy is direct through intellect, not in parables; in Yesodei ha-Torah his mind is described as always prepared, concentrated, and ready for spiritual truth “as the angels are.” This suggests that the highest human cognition for Maimonides is no longer imaginative but purely intellective.

That brings us to the “mind of God.” Maimonides unquestionably treats divine intellect as central, not ornamental. In Guide I.68 and Yesodei ha-Torah 2, God is the knower, the known, and the knowledge itself; His knowledge is not external to Him, and He is pure actuality. Human knowing oscillates between potentiality and actuality; God’s does not. If one wants Maimonides’ full philosophy of mind, this asymmetry is absolutely basic.

But the same Maimonides is relentless about negative theology. In Guide I.56–58 he says that wisdom, life, power, and the like, when predicated of God and humans, have only the same name and nothing else in common; positive attributes do not tell us what God is; negative attributes are the only safe path. So divine “mind” is indispensable but not univocal with created mind. God is not a very large or perfect version of human consciousness. He is the supreme noetic source in a sense that breaks ordinary genus-and-species thinking.

This is why the right formula is not “same kind, different degree,” but “ordered relation without univocity.” Human, angelic, celestial, and divine intellects belong to one hierarchical order of intelligibility, yet the term “intellect” changes in meaning across those levels. That combination—continuity of order with discontinuity of meaning—is one of the hardest and most important features of Maimonides’ thought.

Angelic mind is equally structural. In Yesodei ha-Torah 2, angels are forms without matter, separated from one another not spatially but by degree of spiritual rank and causal priority. Maimonides gives them a tenfold hierarchy, with the lowest level, the ishim, being those angels who speak with prophets and are seen in prophetic visions. He also says that God gives bodies their appropriate forms through angels of the tenth level. Angelic intellect is therefore not a decorative side topic; it is part of the metaphysical machinery connecting divine causality to embodied being and prophetic consciousness.

The Guide makes the link to philosophy explicit: Aristotle’s “intelligences” are, Maimonides says, what Scripture calls “angels.” These separate intellects are intermediaries between the First Cause and existing things and move the spheres. Maimonides differs from Aristotle not on their intellectual status but on whether they are eternal: he insists they are created. In Yesodei ha-Torah 3 he goes further and attributes soul, knowledge, and intellect even to the stars and spheres, which know God, know themselves, and know the angels above them.

That celestial psychology should not be passed over. Maimonides also says the spheres and intelligences are conscious of their actions and choose the objects of their influence, though not in the same manner humans exercise free choice. So between God and human beings there stands an entire field of created minds and quasi-minds. This is a genuinely noetic cosmos, not a universe with one isolated human psychology inside it.

The term “angel,” however, is broader than “separate intellect.” This is one of the most philosophically fertile and easily missed points in the Guide. Maimonides calls “angel” a homonym: it can refer to separate intellectual beings, to spheres, to elements, to natural forces, to ideals perceived in prophetic visions, and even to psychical forces in the individual. He explicitly says that the libidinous disposition can be called an angel, that the imaginative faculty is called “angel,” and that “cherub” is used for the intellectual faculty. Some angels are permanent; others are transient, like individual forces.

This does not mean that Maimonides reduces angels to “mere psychology.” It means something subtler and more ambitious: the same vocabulary of mediated agency runs from cosmic intellects to natural powers to inner faculties. “Angel” names a messenger-function, a delegated efficacy, across ontological levels. That is precisely why angelology belongs inside a Maimonidean philosophy of mind. It shows that human cognition is structurally continuous with a broader order of mediated causation, while still differing in rank from higher created intellects.

Providence then appears as a mental relation, not merely an external divine policy. In Guide III.17 Maimonides says providence is closely connected with intellect: those creatures that receive more of the intellectual influence are under providence in proportion to that reception. In III.51 he radicalizes the point: providence attends rational beings according to the amount of intellect they possess, and even those perfected in knowledge enjoy providence most when their minds are actually turned toward God. When their thought turns elsewhere, they retain knowledge only potentially, and providence correspondingly recedes.

This yields a philosophy not only of mind but of attention. Maimonides’ highest religious ideal is not ritual feeling as such, but sustained intellectual orientation. He describes the highest perfection as “intellectual worship” in which one can speak with others and tend to bodily needs while one’s mind remains with God. In the same spirit, he says that knowing God brings grace, while ignorance brings distance. On his view, attention is not a marginal phenomenon of consciousness; it is the axis on which providence, worship, and perfection turn.

When we come to death and immortality, the layered structure of soul and intellect becomes unavoidable. In Yesodei ha-Torah 4, Maimonides says the higher form of the soul is not a composite of elements and does not require the lower life-principle for its activity. When the body decomposes and the lower, body-dependent psychic life ceases, this higher form is not cut off. Rather, it knows immaterial objects, knows the Creator, and endures forever.

In Teshuvah 8 he makes the eschatology stark: the World to Come has no body, only the souls of the righteous without bodies, like the ministering angels. Their “crowns” are metaphorical and mean the knowledge by which they merited that life; their delight in the radiance of the divine presence means apprehending divine truth beyond what can be grasped in a dark bodily condition. That is not a stray devotional remark. It is the culmination of the entire hierarchy: perfected human mind becoming like angelic mind in a bodiless state.

The Treatise on Resurrection clarifies the famous point of tension. Maimonides firmly affirms bodily resurrection as a principle of Torah, but he insists it is not the final end. The final end is the World to Come. He says the body is a vessel for the soul’s activities and that those activities do not belong to the ultimate state; he also says that separate beings—angels and human intellect—have firmer existence than bodies, and that the inhabitants of the World to Come have separate souls, that is, intellects.

This is the point at which modern interpretation becomes genuinely contested. A strong “intellectualist” reading takes Maimonides to mean more or less what he says in these passages: the perfected human intellect survives bodily death and enjoys incorporeal apprehension of God. A more skeptical recent reading, associated in English scholarship with Josef Stern, argues that Maimonides’ afterlife language may itself be equivocal, so that terms like “intellect,” “form,” or “pleasure” when applied to the afterlife do not straightforwardly extend our ordinary theory of human intellect beyond death. That debate is real, and any rigorous report should acknowledge it.

My own view is that the primary texts force an intellectualist center, even if they leave some skepticism at the edges. Yesodei ha-Torah 4, Teshuvah 8, and the Treatise on Resurrection are too explicit about incorporeality, angelic comparison, and knowledge as postmortem perfection to be dissolved into pure agnosticism. But the skeptical warning is still useful, because Maimonides’ negative theology forbids us from imagining the postmortem intellect too anthropomorphically or too psychologically. The afterlife is described, but not domesticated.

If I compress the reconstruction into a few theses, they are these.

First, Maimonides is fundamentally hylomorphic about embodied human life: the human being is a matter-form composite with one soul and many faculties, and mental life is inseparable from bodily and social conditions.

Second, he is radically intellectualist about perfection: the highest human end is not mere moral decency or emotional piety, but actualized intellect and the knowledge of God. Moral virtue, bodily health, and politics are ordered toward that end.

Third, imagination is ambivalent. It is inferior to intellect as a criterion of truth and a frequent source of error, yet it is indispensable for most prophecy, for symbolic cognition, and for the translation of higher truths into forms graspable by communities.

Fourth, human cognition is not self-contained. It is actualized within a hierarchy of noetic influence mediated by the Active Intellect, the angels, and the celestial order.

Fifth, God, angels, and celestial minds belong inside the philosophy of mind, but not as members of one univocal class. Maimonides builds a graded order of intellects whose terms are related by causal and analogical structure, not by simple sameness of definition.

Sixth, providence in Maimonides is cognitively indexed: it tracks actual intellectual attachment to God. That makes attention, contemplation, and right understanding central to his account of human flourishing.

Seventh, the ultimate destiny of perfected human mind is incorporeal intellectual life, even though the exact metaphysics of personal survival remain debated.

Since you specifically asked for non-Anglophone reception, I made a point of checking Spanish, French, Hebrew, German, and Arabic materials. The Spanish discussions I found emphasize prophecy as the perfected conjunction of rational and imaginative faculties and treat the Active Intellect as the divine intermediary that grounds both knowledge and prophecy. The German material I checked likewise presents the prophet as the highest human type, combining philosophical thinking, reason, and divinely intensified imagination.

The French and Hebrew materials were especially useful for keeping angelology and divine intellect inside the philosophical frame. A French study by Maurice-Ruben Hayoun describes intellect as a more “natural” communication between God and man and explicitly links this to God as supreme intellect; the Hebrew discussion I consulted stresses both sides of Maimonides’ angel doctrine: angels are truly separate incorporeal forms in a causal hierarchy, and scriptural visions of them are prophetic representations rather than empirical sightings. That combination is very close to what the primary texts themselves show.

The Arabic material added something different: not a rival doctrine, but an interpretive lens. A contemporary Arabic essay reads Maimonides’ language of overflow through embodied metaphors of containment and motion. I would not treat that as Maimonides’ own explicit theory, but it does illuminate something real: even a thinker committed to divine incorporeality and negative theology still needs figurative, quasi-spatial language to speak about intellectual influence moving from the divine to the human. That is a useful reminder that anti-anthropomorphism does not eliminate metaphor; it refines its use.

The broad result of looking outside English was, for me, not a new doctrine but a change of emphasis. Non-English receptions more readily keep prophecy, angelology, and cosmic intellect inside the same philosophical field, whereas some Anglophone presentations are tempted to separate “psychology” from those themes. On the evidence of the primary texts, the non-reductive approach is the better one. Maimonides’ philosophy of mind is not merely an ethics of the soul or an epistemology of abstraction. It is a theory of embodied faculties situated within a created hierarchy of minds and ordered toward an irreducibly theological-intellectual end.

So the final answer is this: Maimonides’ philosophy of mind is a philosophy of graded intelligibility. At the lowest level are body, sensation, appetite, and imagination; above these stands rational actualization; above human intellect are angelic and celestial intellects; above all is God, whose knowing is identical with His being. Human excellence consists in ordering the lower toward the higher until intellect becomes as actual as a mortal can make it, imagination becomes a servant rather than a tyrant, and the mind cleaves to God in knowledge. In that system, the mind of God and the minds of angels are not theological digressions. They are the upper reaches of the very ladder on which the human mind is understood.

If you want, I can turn this into a second document that maps the whole view into contemporary philosophy-of-mind categories—faculty psychology, embodiment, intentionality, imagination, consciousness, personal identity, and noetic externalism—while keeping the historical reconstruction intact.
